contentBlocks.original:
ॐ सच्चिदानन्द रूपाय नमोस्तु परमात्मने ।
ज्योतिर्मय स्वरूपाय विश्वमाङ्गल्यमूर्तये ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
ōṃ sachchidānanda rūpāya namōstu paramātmanē ।
jyōtirmaya svarūpāya viśvamāṅgalyamūrtayē ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Oṃ. Salutation to the Supreme Self (Paramātman) of the form of Sat-Cit-Ānanda (Being-Consciousness-Bliss). To the Self-luminous One, to the all-auspicious mūrti of the universe — salutation.
contentBlocks.meanings: oṃ = the sacred syllable; sac-cid-ānanda-rūpāya = to the one of the form (rūpa) of Sat (Being), Cit (Consciousness), Ānanda (Bliss); namaḥ astu = let there be salutation; paramātmane = to the Supreme Self (Paramātman); jyotirmaya-svarūpāya = to the one of self-luminous (jyotirmaya) nature (svarūpa); viśva-māṅgalya-mūrtaye = to the all-auspicious (māṅgalya) form (mūrti) of the universe (viśva).

contentBlocks.original:
प्रकृतिः पञ्च भूतानि ग्रहालोकाः स्वरा स्तधा ।
दिशः कालश्च सर्वेषां सदा कुर्वन्तु मङ्गलम्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
prakṛtiḥ pañcha bhūtāni grahālōkāḥ svarā stadhā ।
diśaḥ kālaścha sarvēṣāṃ sadā kurvantu maṅgaḻam‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: May Nature, the five elements, the planets and the worlds, the musical notes, the directions, and Time — may all of these ever bestow auspiciousness on all beings.
contentBlocks.meanings: prakṛtiḥ = Nature (the primal creative force); pañca-bhūtāni = the five elements (earth, water, fire, air, space); graha-lokāḥ = planets (graha) and worlds (loka); svarāḥ = musical notes (the seven svaras of Indian classical music); tathā = and also; diśaḥ = the directions (the ten digs: four cardinal, four diagonal, up, down); kālaḥ ca = and Time; sarveṣām = for all beings; sadā = always; kurvantu maṅgalam = may they bestow (kurvantu) auspiciousness (maṅgala).

contentBlocks.original:
रत्नाकरा धौतपदां हिमालय किरीटिनीम्‌ ।
ब्रह्मराजर्षि रत्नाढ्यां वन्दे भारत मातरम्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
ratnākarā dhautapadāṃ himālaya kirīṭinīm‌ ।
brahmarājarṣi ratnāḍhyāṃ vandē bhārata mātaram‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: I salute Bhārata-Mātā — Mother India — whose feet are washed by the treasure-oceans, who wears the Himālayas as her crown, and who is adorned with the jewels of Brahmarṣis and Rājarṣis.
contentBlocks.meanings: ratnākara-dhautapadām = she whose feet (pāda) are washed (dhauta) by the treasure-oceans (ratnākara = ocean, literally 'mine of gems'); himālaya-kirīṭinim = she who wears the Himālaya as her crown (kirīṭa); brahma-rājarṣi-ratnāḍhyām = enriched (āḍhya) with the gems (ratna) of Brahmarṣis (seer-sages like Vasiṣṭha, Viśvāmitra) and Rājarṣis (royal sages like Janaka, Daśaratha); vande bhārata-mātaram = I salute (vande) Bhārata-Mātā (Mother Bhārata).

contentBlocks.original:
महेन्द्रो मलयः सह्यो देवतात्मा हिमालयः ।
ध्येयो रैवतको विन्ध्यो गिरिश्चारावलिस्तधा ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
mahēndrō malayaḥ sahyō dēvatātmā himālayaḥ ।
dhyēyō raivatakō vindhyō giriśchārāvalistadhā ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Mahendra, Malaya, Sahya, the divine-souled Himālaya — worthy of meditation — Raivataka, Vindhya, Aravalli, and the other sacred mountain ranges — may they bless us.
contentBlocks.meanings: mahendraḥ = Mahendra (Eastern Ghats, southern portion); malayaḥ = Malaya (the Malaya/Malabar hills of Kerala); sahyaḥ = Sahya (the Western Ghats — the source of many sacred rivers); devatātmā himālayaḥ = the Himālaya whose soul is divine (devatā-ātman), recalled from Kālidāsa's 'devātmā himālayo nāma' (Kumārasambhava 1.1); dhyeyaḥ = worthy of meditation; raivataka = Raivataka (Girnar, sacred hill in Gujarat); vindhyaḥ = Vindhya (the central range); giriś cārāvaliḥ = the mountain and the Aravalli range (Rajasthan); tathā = and others.

contentBlocks.original:
गङ्गा सरस्वती सिन्धुर्‌ ब्रह्मपुत्रश्च गण्डकी ।
कावेरी यमुना रेवा कृष्णागोदा महानदी ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
gaṅgā sarasvatī sindhur‌ brahmaputraścha gaṇḍakī ।
kāvērī yamunā rēvā kṛṣṇāgōdā mahānadī ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Gaṅgā, Sarasvatī, Sindhu, Brahmaputra, Gaṇḍakī, Kāverī, Yamunā, Revā (Narmadā), Kṛṣṇā, Godāvarī — the sacred great rivers of Bhārata.
contentBlocks.meanings: gaṅgā = the Gaṅgā (Ganges) — the most sacred river of India; sarasvatī = the Sarasvatī (now largely underground in Rajasthan; sacred in Vedic tradition); sindhuḥ = the Indus (Sindhu), the river that gave India its name; brahmaputraḥ ca = and the Brahmaputra (son of Brahmā, flowing through Assam); gaṇḍakī = the Gaṇḍakī (Gandak, Nepal-Bihar; source of Śālagrāma stones); kāverī = the Kāverī (sacred river of South India, the Dakṣiṇa-Gaṅgā); yamunā = the Yamunā (associated with Kṛṣṇa and Vṛndāvana); revā = the Revā (Narmadā — the river whose very sight gives liberation); kṛṣṇā = the Kṛṣṇā (Andhra/Karnataka); godā = the Godāvarī (Godā-māyī, sacred to Rāma's south-India sojourn).

contentBlocks.original:
अयोध्या मधुरा माया काशीकाञ्ची अवन्तिका ।
वैशाली द्वारिका ध्येया पुरी तक्षशिला गया ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
ayōdhyā madhurā māyā kāśīkāñchī avantikā ।
vaiśālī dvārikā dhyēyā purī takṣaśilā gayā ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Ayodhyā, Mathurā, Māyā (Haridvāra), Kāśī, Kāñcī, Avantikā (Ujjain), Vaiśālī, Dvārikā — worthy of meditation — Purī (Jagannātha), Takṣaśilā, Gayā.
contentBlocks.meanings: ayodhyā = birthplace of Śrī Rāma, the first of the seven sacred cities; mathurā = birthplace of Śrī Kṛṣṇa; māyā = Māyāpurī (Haridvāra — the door to heaven); kāśī = Vārāṇasī, the city of Śiva where dying grants liberation; kāñcī = Kāñcīpuram, one of the four dhāmas and seat of Śaṃkarācārya; avantikā = Ujjain (Mahākāla Śiva's city, site of the Mahāparva Kumbha Melā); vaiśālī = the ancient republic of the Licchavis, birthplace of Mahāvīra; dvārikā = Dvārakā, Kṛṣṇa's western capital and seat of the Śāradā-pīṭha; purī = Purī (Jagannātha's dhāma); takṣaśilā = Taxila (ancient university); gayā = the liberation-granting tīrtha of Gayā (Bodh Gayā / Pitṛ-Gayā).

contentBlocks.original:
प्रयागः पाटली पुत्रं विजयानगरं महत्‌ ।
इन्द्रप्रस्धं सोमनाधः तधामृतसरः प्रियम्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
prayāgaḥ pāṭalī putraṃ vijayānagaraṃ mahat‌ ।
indraprasdhaṃ sōmanādhaḥ tadhāmṛtasaraḥ priyam‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Prayāga, Pāṭaliputra (Patna), the great Vijayanagara, Indraprastha (Delhi), Somanātha, and the beloved Amṛtasara (Amritsar) — all worthy of remembrance and meditation.
contentBlocks.meanings: prayāgaḥ = Prayāga (Allahabad/Prayāgarāja, confluence of Gaṅgā-Yamunā-Sarasvatī and site of the Kumbha Melā); pāṭali-putram = Pāṭaliputra (ancient Magadha capital, modern Patna); vijayanagaram mahat = the great Vijayanagara (the empire and its capital, seat of Kṛṣṇadevarāya and the last great Hindu imperial city); indraprastham = Indraprastha (Pāṇḍava capital, modern Delhi); somanāthaḥ = the Somanātha temple (Śiva's first jyotirliṅga, Gujarat); tathā amṛtasaraḥ priyam = and the beloved Amṛtasara (Amritsar, the Golden Temple, holiest site of the Sikh tradition).

contentBlocks.original:
चतुर्वेदाः पुराणानि सर्वोपनिषदस्तधा ।
रामायणं भारतं च गीता षड्दर्शनानि च ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
chaturvēdāḥ purāṇāni sarvōpaniṣadastadhā ।
rāmāyaṇaṃ bhārataṃ cha gītā ṣaḍdarśanāni cha ॥
contentBlocks.translation: The four Vedas, the Purāṇas, all the Upaniṣads, the Rāmāyaṇa, the Mahābhārata, the Gītā, and the six philosophical systems (Darśanas) — this supreme treasury of knowledge.
contentBlocks.meanings: catur-vedāḥ = the four Vedas (Ṛgveda, Yajurveda, Sāmaveda, Atharvaveda); purāṇāni = the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas and Upapurāṇas; sarva-upaniṣadas = all the Upaniṣads (108 canonical, others besides); tathā = and; rāmāyaṇam bhāratam ca = Rāmāyaṇa and Mahābhārata (the two Itihāsas); gītā = the Bhagavad Gītā; ṣaḍ-darśanāni ca = and the six philosophical systems (Sāṃkhya, Yoga, Nyāya, Vaiśeṣika, Mīmāṃsā, Vedānta).

contentBlocks.original:
जैनागमा स्त्रिपिटका गुरुग्रन्धः सतां गिरः ।
एषः ज्ञाननिधिः श्रेष्ठः हृदि सर्वदा ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
jaināgamā stripiṭakā gurugrandhaḥ satāṃ giraḥ ।
ēṣaḥ jñānanidhiḥ śrēṣṭhaḥ hṛdi sarvadā ॥
contentBlocks.translation: The Jaina Āgamas, the Tripiṭaka (Buddhist canon), the Gurū Granth (Sikh scripture), and the words of the holy ones — this supreme treasury of knowledge: may it ever dwell in every heart.
contentBlocks.meanings: jaina-āgamāḥ = the Jaina Āgamas (canonical scriptures of the Jaina tradition, divided into Āgamas, Upāṅgas, etc.); tri-piṭakā = the Tripiṭaka (the three-basket Buddhist canon: Vinaya, Sutta, Abhidhamma); guru-granthaḥ = the Gurū Granth Sāhib (the Sikh sacred scripture); satām giraḥ = the words (gir = utterance) of the saintly (sat); eṣaḥ jñāna-nidhiḥ śreṣṭhaḥ = this supreme (śreṣṭha) treasury (nidhi) of knowledge (jñāna); hṛdi sarvadā = may it always dwell in the heart.

contentBlocks.original:
अरुन्धत्यनसूय च सावित्री जानकी सती ।
द्रौपदी कण्णगी गार्गी मीरा दुर्गावती तधा ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
arundhatyanasūya cha sāvitrī jānakī satī ।
draupadī kaṇṇagī gārgī mīrā durgāvatī tadhā ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Arundhatī, Anasūyā, Sāvitrī, Jānakī (Sītā), Satī, Draupadī, Kaṇṇagī, Gārgī, Mīrā, Durgāvatī — these holy mother-figures are to be venerated.
contentBlocks.meanings: arundhatī = the sage-wife Arundhatī (Vasiṣṭha's wife, model of chastity; also the star Alcor); anasūyā = Anasūyā (wife of sage Atri, who transformed the Trimūrti with her chastity); sāvitrī = Sāvitrī (who outwitted Yama to rescue her husband Satyavān); jānakī = Jānakī (Sītā, daughter of Janaka); satī = Satī (Śiva's first consort, who self-immolated); draupadī = Draupadī (wife of the five Pāṇḍavas, protected by Kṛṣṇa); kaṇṇagī = Kaṇṇagi (Tamil heroine of the Cilappatikāram, who destroyed Madurai for injustice); gārgī = Gārgī Vācaknavī (the Vedic female philosopher who challenged Yājñavalkya); mīrā = Mīrābāī (Rājput saint-poet devoted to Kṛṣṇa); durgāvatī = Durgāvatī (the warrior queen of Gondwana who died fighting the Mughals).

contentBlocks.original:
लक्ष्मी रहल्या चॆन्नम्मा रुद्रमाम्बा सुविक्रमा ।
निवेदिता शारदा च प्रणम्याः मातृदेवताः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
lakṣmī rahalyā chennammā rudramāmbā suvikramā ।
nivēditā śāradā cha praṇamyāḥ mātṛdēvatāḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Lakṣmī (Bāī), Ahalyā (Bāī), Chennammā, the valorous Rudramāmbā, Sister Niveditā, Śāradā (Devī) — these mother-goddesses are worthy of salutation.
contentBlocks.meanings: lakṣmī = Lakṣmī Bāī (the Rānī of Jhānsī, who died fighting the British in 1858); rahalyā = Ahalyābāī Holkar (Maratha queen of Indore, renowned for justice and temple-building); chennammā = Keladi Chennammā or Kittūr Chennammā (warrior queens who resisted colonial rule); rudramāmbā suvikramā = Rudramadevi (Kākatīya queen of Warangal, 13th c., who ruled disguised as a man), the valiant (suvikramā); niveditā = Sister Niveditā (Margaret Noble, Irish disciple of Vivekānanda who served India); śāradā ca = and Śāradā (Śāradā Devī, wife of Rāmakṛṣṇa Paramahaṃsa); praṇamyāḥ mātṛ-devatāḥ = these mother-goddesses worthy of salutation (praṇamya).

contentBlocks.original:
श्रीरामो भरतः कृष्णो भीष्मो धर्म स्तधार्जुनः ।
मार्कण्डेया हरिश्चन्द्रः प्रह्लादो नारदो ध्रुवः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
śrīrāmō bharataḥ kṛṣṇō bhīṣmō dharma stadhārjunaḥ ।
mārkaṇḍēyā hariśchandraḥ prahlādō nāradō dhruvaḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Śrī Rāma, Bharata, Kṛṣṇa, Bhīṣma, Dharmarāja (Yudhiṣṭhira), Arjuna, Mārkaṇḍeya, Hariścandra, Prahlāda, Nārada — these luminaries are worthy of perpetual remembrance.
contentBlocks.meanings: śrī-rāmaḥ = Śrī Rāma (the ideal king and avatāra); bharataḥ = Bharata (Rāma's devoted brother, ideal of selfless loyalty); kṛṣṇaḥ = Śrī Kṛṣṇa (the Gītā-teacher and divine friend of the Pāṇḍavas); bhīṣmaḥ = Bhīṣma (the Mahābhārata patriarch, model of celibate devotion); dharmaḥ = Dharmarāja (Yudhiṣṭhira, the eldest Pāṇḍava, embodiment of truth and justice); tathā arjunaḥ = and Arjuna (the archer-warrior, Kṛṣṇa's disciple); mārkaṇḍeyaḥ = Mārkaṇḍeya (the sage who conquered death through devotion to Śiva); hariścandraḥ = Hariścandra (the truth-keeping king who endured extreme trials); prahlādaḥ = Prahlāda (the child-devotee of Viṣṇu who defied his demon father); nāradaḥ = Nārada (the cosmic wandering sage-musician, devotee and teacher).

contentBlocks.original:
हनुमान्‌ जनको व्यासो वशिष्ठश्च शुको बलिः ।
दधीचि विश्वकर्माणौ पृधु वाल्मीकि भार्गवाः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
hanumān‌ janakō vyāsō vaśiṣṭhaścha śukō baliḥ ।
dadhīchi viśvakarmāṇau pṛdhu vālmīki bhārgavāḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Hanumān, Janaka, Vyāsa, Vasiṣṭha, Śuka, Bali, Dadhīci, Viśvakarmā, Pṛthu, Vālmīki, Bhārgava (Paraśurāma) — these are the luminaries of sacred tradition.
contentBlocks.meanings: hanumān = Hanumān (the devoted servant of Rāma, model of selfless service); janakaḥ = Janaka (the philosopher-king of Mithilā, Sītā's father); vyāsaḥ = Vyāsa (Vedavyāsa, who compiled the Vedas and composed the Mahābhārata and Purāṇas); vasiṣṭhaḥ ca = and Vasiṣṭha (the royal preceptor of the Solar dynasty); śukaḥ = Śuka (Vyāsa's son, who narrated the Bhāgavata to King Parīkṣit); baliḥ = Bali (the generous demon-king who gave his all to Vāmana); dadhīci = Dadhīci (the sage who sacrificed his bones for the gods' weapons); viśvakarmā = Viśvakarmā (the divine architect, patron deity of craftsmen); pṛthuḥ = Pṛthu (the first king, who milked the earth for her bounty); vālmīki = Vālmīki (the first poet, author of the Rāmāyaṇa); bhārgavaḥ = Bhārgava (Paraśurāma, the warrior-sage of the Bhṛgu lineage).

contentBlocks.original:
भगीरधश्चैकलव्यो मनुर्धन्वन्तरिस्तधा ।
शिबिश्च रन्तिदेवश्च पुराणोद्गीत कीर्तयः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
bhagīradhaśchaikalavyō manurdhanvantaristadhā ।
śibiścha rantidēvaścha purāṇōdgīta kīrtayaḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Bhagīratha, Ekalavya, Manu, Dhanvantari, Śibi, and Rantideva — these are the ones whose glories are eternally sung in the Purāṇas.
contentBlocks.meanings: bhagīrathaḥ = Bhagīratha (the king who performed tapas to bring the Gaṅgā from heaven to earth); ca ekalavyaḥ = and Ekalavya (the tribal archer who offered his thumb to Droṇa as guru-dakṣiṇā — model of self-transcending devotion); manuḥ = Manu (the first lawgiver, progenitor of humanity, who survived the great flood); dhanvantariḥ = Dhanvantari (the divine physician who emerged from the churning of the ocean bearing the amṛta and the science of Āyurveda); tathā = and; śibiḥ ca rantidevaḥ ca = Śibi (the king who offered his own flesh to save a dove from a hawk) and Rantideva (who gave away his last food and water to a beggar and saw all gods in him); purāṇa-udgīta-kīrtayaḥ = those whose glories are sung (udgīta) in the Purāṇas.

contentBlocks.original:
बुद्धोजिनेन्द्रा गोरक्षः तिरुवल्लुवरस्तधा ।
नायन्मारालवाराश्च कम्बश्च बसवेश्वरः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
buddhōjinēndrā gōrakṣaḥ tiruvaḻḻuvarastadhā ।
nāyanmārālavārāścha kambaścha basavēśvaraḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Buddha, the Jina (Mahāvīra), Gorakṣa (Nātha), Tiruvaḷḷuvar, the Nāyaṉmārs (Śaiva saints), the Āḷvārs (Vaiṣṇava saints), Kamban, and Basaveśvara — luminaries of devotion and wisdom across traditions.
contentBlocks.meanings: buddhaḥ = Gautama Buddha (founder of Buddhism, 5th c. BCE); jinendraḥ = Jinendra (Mahāvīra, the 24th Tīrthaṃkara of Jainism); gorakṣaḥ = Gorakṣanātha (the great Nātha-sampradāya yogi); tiruvaḷḷuvara = Tiruvaḷḷuvar (Tamil poet-sage, author of the Tirukkuṛaḷ, c. 1st-5th c. CE); nāyaṉmār āḷvārāś ca = the 63 Nāyaṉmārs (Śaiva Tamil saints) and 12 Āḷvārs (Vaiṣṇava Tamil saints); kamba = Kamban (12th c. Tamil poet, author of the Kamba Rāmāyaṇam); basaveśvaraḥ = Basaveśvara (12th c. Kannada saint and social reformer, founder of the Vīraśaiva/Liṅgāyat tradition).

contentBlocks.original:
देवलो रविदासश्च कबीरो गुरुनानकः ।
नरसिस्तुलसीदासो दशमेशो दृढव्रतः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
dēvalō ravidāsaścha kabīrō gurunānakaḥ ।
narasistulasīdāsō daśamēśō dṛḍhavrataḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Devala, Ravidāsa, Kabīr, Gurū Nānak, Narasī (Narasiṃha Mehtā), Tulasīdāsa, the firm-vowed Daśameśa (Gurū Gobind Singh) — these saints are revered across all of Bhārata.
contentBlocks.meanings: devalaḥ = Devala (an ancient Vedic sage; or Devala Jāṭī, a medieval saint?); ravidāsaḥ ca = and Ravidāsa (15th c. Dalit saint of the Bhakti movement, disciple tradition); kabīraḥ = Kabīr (the weaver-mystic of Vārāṇasī, 15th c., revered by Hindus, Muslims, Sikhs); guru-nānakaḥ = Gurū Nānak (1469–1539 CE, founder of the Sikh faith); narasiḥ = Narasī (Narasiṃha Mehtā, 15th c. Gujarati saint-poet, called 'ādi kavi' of Gujarati literature); tulasīdāsaḥ = Tulasīdāsa (16th c., author of the Rāmacaritamānasa in Awadhi); daśameśaḥ dṛḍha-vrataḥ = the tenth Gurū (Gobind Singh, 1666–1708), of firm vow (dṛḍha-vrata).

contentBlocks.original:
श्रीमत्‌ शङ्करदेवश्च बन्धू सायणमाधवौ ।
ज्ञानेश्वर स्तुकारामो रामदासः पुरन्दरः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
śrīmat‌ śaṅkaradēvaścha bandhū sāyaṇamādhavau ।
jñānēśvara stukārāmō rāmadāsaḥ purandaraḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Śrī Śaṃkaradeva, the brothers Sāyaṇa and Mādhava, Jñāneśvara, Tukārāma, Rāmadāsa, Purandaradāsa — saints and scholars of the eastern, western, and southern traditions.
contentBlocks.meanings: śrīmat śaṃkaradeva = Śaṃkaradeva (1449–1568 CE, the great Assamese Vaiṣṇava saint-reformer and playwright who shaped Assamese culture); bandhu sāyaṇa-mādhavau = brothers Sāyaṇa and Mādhava (14th c.: Mādhavācārya, the philosopher and founder of the Vijayanagara kingdom's ideological framework, and his brother Sāyaṇa, the peerless Vedic commentator); jñāneśvaraḥ = Jñāneśvara (Jñāndev, 13th c. Marathi saint, author of the Jñāneśvarī commentary on the Gītā); tukārāmaḥ = Tukārāma (17th c. Marathi saint-poet of the Vārkarī tradition); rāmadāsaḥ = Rāmadāsa (Samartha Rāmadāsa, Śivājī's guru, 17th c.); purandaraḥ = Purandaradāsa (16th c., 'the father of Carnatic music,' Karnataka saint).

contentBlocks.original:
विरजा सहजानन्दो रामासन्द्स्तधा महान्‌ ।
वितरस्तु सदैवैते दैवीं सद्गुण सम्पदम्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
virajā sahajānandō rāmāsandstadhā mahān‌ ।
vitarastu sadaivaitē daivīṃ sadguṇa sampadam‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Viraja, Sahajānanda, Rāmānanda, and other great ones — may these always bestow upon us the divine wealth of noble qualities.
contentBlocks.meanings: virajā = Viraja (Virajānanda, teacher of Dayānanda Sarasvatī); sahajānandaḥ = Sahajānanda (Swāmīnārāyaṇa, 1781–1830, founder of the Swāmīnārāyaṇa Sampradāya in Gujarat); rāmā-sandasthā mahān = the great Rāmānanda (14th-15th c., founder of the Rāmānandī Vaiṣṇava tradition that inspired Kabīr, Tulsī, Ravidāsa and others); vitarantu = may they bestow (vitarantu = let them distribute/give); sadaiva ete = these always; daivīṃ sad-guṇa-sampadam = the divine (daivī) wealth (sampada) of good qualities (sad-guṇa).

contentBlocks.original:
भरतर्षिः कालिदासः श्रीभोजो जकणस्तधा ।
सूरदासस्त्यागराजो रसखानश्च सत्कविः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
bharatarṣiḥ kāḻidāsaḥ śrībhōjō jakaṇastadhā ।
sūradāsastyāgarājō rasakhānaścha satkaviḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Bharatarṣi (Bharata Muni), Kālidāsa, Śrī Bhoja, Jakaṇa, Sūradāsa, Tyāgarāja, Rasakhāna — these noble poets are to be remembered.
contentBlocks.meanings: bharata-ṛṣiḥ = Bharata Muni (author of the Nāṭyaśāstra, the foundational text of Indian performing arts — dance, music, and drama); kālidāsaḥ = Kālidāsa (the supreme Sanskrit poet, author of Abhijñānaśākuntala, Meghadūta, Kumārasambhava, Raghuvamśa, and the Pañchastavya we have been translating); śrī-bhojaḥ = Bhoja (11th c. polymath king of Dhārā, author of over 80 works, patron of scholars); jakaṇaḥ = Jakkaṇācārya (12th c. Kannada poet); sūradāsaḥ = Sūradāsa (16th c. Hindi Kṛṣṇa-bhakti poet, blind saint, author of the Sūrasāgara); tyāgarājaḥ = Tyāgarāja (1767–1847, the great Carnatic composer, one of the Trimūrti of Carnatic music); rasa-khānaḥ = Rasakhāna (Syed Ibrahim Khan, 16th c. Muslim devotional poet who wrote passionate Kṛṣṇa-bhakti poetry in Braj Bhasha).

contentBlocks.original:
रविवर्मा भारतखण्डे भाग्यचन्द्रः स भूपतिः ।
कलावन्तश्च विख्याताः स्मरणीय निरन्तरम्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
ravivarmā bhāratakhaṇḍē bhāgyachandraḥ sa bhūpatiḥ ।
kalāvantaścha vikhyātāḥ smaraṇīya nirantaram‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Ravi Varma, Bhāgyacandra the king, and other famous artists of Bhārata — these luminaries of art are to be remembered perpetually.
contentBlocks.meanings: ravi-varmā = Raja Ravi Varma (1848–1906, the Kerala painter who created the iconic visual imagery of Hindu deities and mythological scenes for all of modern India); bhārata-khaṇḍe = in the land of Bhārata; bhāgya-candraḥ sa bhūpatiḥ = that king (bhūpati) Bhāgyacandra (the 18th c. Meitei king of Manipur who patronized classical dance and created the Rāsa Līlā dance tradition); kalāvantaś ca = and the artists (kalāvanta); vikhyātāḥ = famous; smaraṇīyā nirantaram = worthy of perpetual (nirantara) remembrance (smaraṇīya).

contentBlocks.original:
अगस्त्यः कम्बुकौण्डिन्यौ राजेन्द्रश्चोलवंशजः ।
अशोकः पुष्यमित्रश्च खारवेलाः सुनीतिमान्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
agastyaḥ kambukauṇḍinyau rājēndraśchōlavaṃśajaḥ ।
aśōkaḥ puṣyamitraścha khāravēlāḥ sunītimān‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Agastya, Kambu, Kauṇḍinya, Rājendra of the Cōḷa dynasty, Aśoka, Puṣyamitra, and righteous Khāravela — these are the great rulers and sages of history.
contentBlocks.meanings: agastyaḥ = Agastya (the Vedic sage who crossed the Vindhyas to bring Vedic civilization to South India and Southeast Asia); kambu = Kambu Svāyambhuva (legendary founder of the Khmer civilization of Cambodia); kauṇḍinyau = Kauṇḍinya (the Brahmin who is said to have founded the Funan kingdom in Southeast Asia by marrying a local queen Soma); rājendraḥ cola-vaṃśajaḥ = Rājendra Cōḷa I (11th c., who sent a naval expedition to Southeast Asia and is buried near modern Chennai); aśokaḥ = Aśoka (273–232 BCE, the Maurya emperor who spread Buddhism across Asia); puṣyamitraḥ ca = and Puṣyamitra Śuṅga (who re-established Vedic tradition after the Mauryas); khāravelāḥ = Khāravela (1st c. BCE Jain king of Kalinga, renowned for justice and piety).

contentBlocks.original:
चाणक्य चन्द्रगुप्तौ च विक्रमः शालिवाहनः ।
समुद्र गुप्तः श्री हर्षःशैलेन्द्रो बप्परावलः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
chāṇakya chandraguptau cha vikramaḥ śālivāhanaḥ ।
samudra guptaḥ śrī harṣaḥśailēndrō bapparāvalaḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Cāṇakya, Candragupta, Vikramāditya, Śālivāhana, Samudragupta, Śrī Harṣa, Śailendra, Bapparāvala — great rulers who shaped Bhārata's destiny.
contentBlocks.meanings: cāṇakya = Cāṇakya (Kauṭilya, 4th c. BCE, the strategic mastermind of the Maurya Empire and author of the Arthaśāstra); candraguptau ca = and Candragupta (Maurya, the first pan-Indian emperor, 322–298 BCE); vikramaḥ = Vikramāditya (the legendary king of Ujjain, patron of the Navratnas including Kālidāsa); śālivāhanaḥ = Śālivāhana (the Sātavāhana king of Paiṭhān who defeated the Śakas); samudra-guptaḥ = Samudragupta (4th c. CE, the Gupta emperor-conqueror and musician); śrī harṣaḥ = Śrī Harṣa (7th c., king of Kanauj, patron of learning, author of Priyadarśikā); śailendraḥ = Śailendra (8th-9th c., the Indonesian dynasty that built Borobudur); bapparāvalaḥ = Bapparāvala/Bappa Rāwal (8th c. founder of the Mewar-Rājput dynasty).

contentBlocks.original:
लाचित्‌ भास्करवर्माच यशोधर्मा च हूणजित्‌ ।
श्रीकृष्णदेवरायश्च ललितादित्य उद्बलः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
lāchit‌ bhāskaravarmācha yaśōdharmā cha hūṇajit‌ ।
śrīkṛṣṇadēvarāyaścha lalitāditya udbalaḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Lāchit (Borphukan), Bhāskaravarman, Yaśodharman the conqueror of the Huṇas, Śrī Kṛṣṇadevarāya, and mighty Lalitāditya — these heroes defended Bhārata's dharma and sovereignty.
contentBlocks.meanings: lācit = Lāchit Borphukan (17th c., the Ahom general of Assam who defeated the Mughal army at the Battle of Saraighat in 1671 — Assam's greatest military hero); bhāskaravarmā ca = and Bhāskaravarman (7th c. king of Kāmarūpa, Assam, ally of Harṣa); yaśodharman = Yaśodharman (6th c. king of Malwa who stopped the Hūṇa invasions); hūṇajit = conqueror of the Hūṇas (Huns); śrī-kṛṣṇadevarāyaś ca = and Śrī Kṛṣṇadevarāya (1509–1529, the greatest king of Vijayanagara, patron of Telugu literature, warrior and poet); lalitādityaḥ udbalaḥ = Lalitāditya (8th c. king of Kashmir, mighty [udbala] conqueror who extended his empire from Central Asia to Bengal).

contentBlocks.original:
मुसुनूरि नायका तौ प्रतापः शिवभूपतिः ।
रणजित्‌ सिंह इत्येते वीरा विख्यात विक्रमाः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
musunūri nāyakā tau pratāpaḥ śivabhūpatiḥ ।
raṇajit‌ siṃha ityētē vīrā vikhyāta vikramāḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: The Musunūri Nāyakas (both brothers), Mahārāṇā Pratāpa, Śivabhūpati (Śivājī), Raṇajit Singh — these renowned warriors of famous valor are revered across Bhārata.
contentBlocks.meanings: musunūri-nāyakā tau = those two Musunūri Nāyakas (Prolaya Nāyaka and Kapaya Nāyaka, 14th c. Telugu chiefs who liberated Andhra from the Turkic Sultanate of Delhi); pratāpaḥ = Mahārāṇā Pratāpa (16th c. Rājput king of Mewar who refused to submit to Akbar and fought the Battle of Haldighāṭī); śiva-bhūpatiḥ = Śiva-bhūpati = Śivājī Mahārāja (17th c., founder of the Maratha Empire, champion of Hindu svarājya); raṇajit-siṃha = Mahārāja Raṇajit Singh (1780–1839, founder of the Sikh Empire of Punjab, who held back the British and the Afghans); ity ete vīrāḥ vikhyāta-vikramāḥ = these renowned (vikhyāta) warriors (vīra) of famous valor (vikrama).

contentBlocks.original:
वैज्ञानिकाश्च कपिलः कणादः शुश्रत स्तधा ।
चरको भास्कराचार्यो वराहमिहरः सुधीः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
vaijñānikāścha kapilaḥ kaṇādaḥ śuśrata stadhā ।
charakō bhāskarāchāryō varāhamiharaḥ sudhīḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Among scientists: Kapila, Kaṇāda, Suśruta, Caraka, Bhāskarācārya, and the wise Varāhamihira — these illuminate the heritage of Indian science.
contentBlocks.meanings: vaijñānikāś ca = and the scientists/scholars (vaijñānika); kapilaḥ = Kapila (the founder of the Sāṃkhya philosophical system, one of India's first systematic thinkers about the structure of reality); kaṇādaḥ = Kaṇāda (founder of Vaiśeṣika — the atomic theory of matter, c. 6th c. BCE); suśrutaḥ = Suśruta (the father of surgery, author of Suśruta Saṃhitā, c. 6th c. BCE); tathā = and; carakaḥ = Caraka (author of Caraka Saṃhitā, foundational text of Āyurveda); bhāskarācāryaḥ = Bhāskarācārya (12th c. mathematician, author of Līlāvatī and Siddhāntaśiromaṇi, who calculated planetary positions and developed early calculus concepts); varāhamihiraḥ sudhīḥ = the wise Varāhamihira (6th c., astronomer-mathematician who compiled the Pañca-Siddhāntikā).

contentBlocks.original:
नागार्जुनो भरद्वाजः आर्यभट्टो वसुर्भुधः ।
ध्येयो वेङ्कटरामश्च विज्ञा रामानुजादयः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
nāgārjunō bharadvājaḥ āryabhaṭṭō vasurbhudhaḥ ।
dhyēyō vēṅkaṭarāmaścha vijñā rāmānujādayaḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Nāgārjuna, Bharadvāja, Āryabhaṭṭa, Vasu, and among the scientists — Veṅkaṭarāma, Rāmānuja and others worthy of meditation.
contentBlocks.meanings: nāgārjunaḥ = Nāgārjuna (2nd c. CE, founder of the Mādhyamaka school of Buddhism and an alchemist of the Rasāyana tradition); bharadvājaḥ = Bharadvāja (the sage to whom the Vimāna Śāstra on aircraft is attributed; also author of Dharmasūtras); āryabhaṭṭaḥ = Āryabhaṭṭa (5th c., the mathematician-astronomer who calculated π, the circumference of the earth, and first proposed the earth rotates on its axis); vasuḥ = Jagadish Chandra Bose (or another named Vasu); dhyeyaḥ = worthy of meditation; veṅkaṭarāmaś ca = and Veṅkaṭarāma (C.V. Raman, Nobel Prize 1930, discoverer of the Raman Effect); vijñā rāmānujādayaḥ = the scientist (vijña) Rāmānuja (Srinivasa Ramanujan, the mathematical genius) and others.

contentBlocks.original:
रामकृष्णो दयानन्दो रवीन्द्रो राममोहनः ।
रामतीर्धो रविन्दश्च विवेकानन्द उड्यशाः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
rāmakṛṣṇō dayānandō ravīndrō rāmamōhanaḥ ।
rāmatīrdhō ravindaścha vivēkānanda uḍyaśāḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Rāmakṛṣṇa (Paramahaṃsa), Dayānanda (Sarasvatī), Rabīndranātha (Tagore), Rāma Mohan (Roy), Rāmatīrtha, Rabīndra (again implied) — and the illustrious Vivekānanda — these are the luminaries of India's modern renaissance.
contentBlocks.meanings: rāmakṛṣṇaḥ = Śrī Rāmakṛṣṇa Paramahaṃsa (1836–1886, the mystic of Dakṣiṇeśvara who experienced all religions as paths to the same God); dayānandaḥ = Swāmī Dayānanda Sarasvatī (1824–1883, founder of the Ārya Samāj, who championed Vedic revivalism and social reform); ravīndraḥ = Rabīndranātha Tagore (1861–1941, Nobel Prize for Literature 1913, author of Gītāñjali, composer of Jana Gana Mana); rāmamohan = Rāja Rām Mohan Roy (1772–1833, reformer who fought against sati and founded the Brahmo Samāj); rāmatīrthaḥ = Swāmī Rāma Tīrtha (1873–1906, Vedānta teacher who carried the message to Japan and America); vivekānanda = Swāmī Vivekānanda (1863–1902, Rāmakṛṣṇa's chief disciple, who represented India at the Parliament of World Religions in Chicago, 1893).

contentBlocks.original:
दादाभायी गोपबन्धुः तिलको गान्धिरादृताः ।
रमणो मालवीयश्च श्री सुब्रह्मण्य भारती ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
dādābhāyī gōpabandhuḥ tilakō gāndhirādṛtāḥ ।
ramaṇō mālavīyaścha śrī subrahmaṇya bhāratī ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Dādābhāī (Naorojī), Gopabandhu (Dās), Tilak, the revered Gandhi, Ramaṇa (Maharṣi), Mālavīya, and Śrī Subrahmaṇya Bhāratī — these are India's great freedom-fighters and spiritual leaders.
contentBlocks.meanings: dādābhāyī = Dādābhāī Naorojī (1825–1917, the 'Grand Old Man of India,' first Indian member of the British Parliament, who formulated the 'drain theory' of colonial exploitation); gopabandhuḥ = Gopabandhu Dāsa (1877–1928, Odiyan patriot-saint who devoted his life to educating the poor); tilakaḥ = Bāl Gaṅgādhar Tilak (1856–1920, called 'Lokamānya' — revered by all people; who declared 'Svarāja is my birthright'); gāndhiḥ āṛtāḥ = the revered (āṛta) Gandhi (Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi, 1869–1948); ramaṇaḥ = Ramaṇa Maharṣi (1879–1950, the Advaitic sage of Aruṇācala); mālavīyaś ca = and Mālavīya (Madan Mohan Mālavīya, 1861–1946, founder of BHU); śrī-subrahmaṇya-bhāratī = Subramaṇya Bhāratī (1882–1921, Tamil revolutionary poet).

contentBlocks.original:
सुभाषः प्रणवानन्दः क्रान्तिवीरो विनायकः ।
ठक्करो भीमरावश्च पुलेनारायणो गुरुः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
subhāṣaḥ praṇavānandaḥ krāntivīrō vināyakaḥ ।
ṭhakkarō bhīmarāvaścha pulēnārāyaṇō guruḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Subhāṣa (Chandra Bose), Praṇavānanda, the revolutionary Vināyaka (Savarkar), Ṭhakkara Bāpā, Bhīmarāva (Ambedkar), and Nārāyaṇa Guru — these are the builders of modern India.
contentBlocks.meanings: subhāṣaḥ = Subhāṣ Candra Bose (1897–1945, Netaji, who formed the Indian National Army to fight the British militarily); praṇavānandaḥ = Swāmī Praṇavānanda (1896–1941, Bengal saint who founded the Bhārat Sevāśrama Saṅgha); krantivīraḥ vināyakaḥ = the revolutionary hero (krantivīra) Vināyaka (V.D. Savarkar, 1883–1966, revolutionary and ideologue); ṭhakkaraḥ = Ṭhakkara Bāpā (1869–1951, social worker for tribal welfare); bhīmarāvaś ca = and Bhīmarāva (B.R. Ambedkar, 1891–1956, the Dalit leader and principal architect of the Indian Constitution); pule-nārāyaṇo guruḥ = Nārāyaṇa Guru (1856–1928, Kerala reformer-saint of the Ezhava community who preached 'one caste, one religion, one God').

contentBlocks.original:
सङ्घशक्तिः प्रणेतारौ केशवो माधवश्तधा ।
स्मरणीया सदैवैते नवचैतन्यदायकाः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
saṅghaśaktiḥ praṇētārau kēśavō mādhavaśtadhā ।
smaraṇīyā sadaivaitē navachaitanyadāyakāḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: The two founders of Saṅgha-Śakti — Keśava (Hedgewar) and Mādhava (Golwalkar) — are always worthy of remembrance as givers of new consciousness and vitality.
contentBlocks.meanings: saṅgha-śaktiḥ = the power (śakti) of organization/union (saṅgha); praṇetārau = the two founders/leaders (praṇetā = one who leads forward); keśavaḥ = Keśava (Dr. Keśav Baliram Hedgewar, 1889–1940, founder of the RSS in 1925); mādhavaḥ tathā = and Mādhava (M.S. Golwalkar, 'Guruji,' 1906–1973, second Sarsanghcālakaḥ of RSS); smaraṇīyā sadaiva ete = these are always to be remembered; nava-caitanya-dāyakāḥ = givers (dāyaka) of new (nava) consciousness/life-force (caitanya).

contentBlocks.original:
अनुक्ता ये भक्ताः प्रभुचरण संसक्त हृदयाः ।
अविज्ञाता वीराः अधिसमरमुद्ध्वस्तरिपवः ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
anuktā yē bhaktāḥ prabhucharaṇa saṃsakta hṛdayāḥ ।
avijñātā vīrāḥ adhisamaramuddhvastaripavaḥ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: Those devotees not named here whose hearts clung to the Lord's feet, those unnamed heroes who crushed enemies on the battlefield —
contentBlocks.meanings: anuktāḥ = those not spoken of/unnamed (a-ukta); ye bhaktāḥ = those devotees (bhakta); prabhu-caraṇa saṃsakta-hṛdayāḥ = whose hearts (hṛdaya) were attached/clinging (saṃsakta) to the Lord's (prabhu) feet (caraṇa); avijñātā vīrāḥ = the unknown (avijñāta) heroes (vīra); adhi-samaram = on the battlefield (samara = battle; adhi = over/upon); uddhvasta-ripavaḥ = those who utterly destroyed (uddhvasta) enemies (ripu).

contentBlocks.original:
समाजोद्धर्तारः सुहितकरविज्ञान निपुणाः ।
नम स्तेभ्यो भूयात्‌ सकल सुजनेभ्यः प्रतिदिनम्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
samājōddhartāraḥ suhitakaravijñāna nipuṇāḥ ।
nama stēbhyō bhūyāt‌ sakala sujanēbhyaḥ pratidinam‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: The uplifters of society, those skilled in beneficial knowledge and science — may salutations be offered to them and to all good people, every single day.
contentBlocks.meanings: samāja-uddhartāraḥ = the uplifters (uddhartā) of society (samāja); su-hita-kara-vijñāna-nipuṇāḥ = those skilled (nipuṇa) in knowledge (vijñāna) that does genuine good/welfare (suhitakara = causing well-being); namaḥ tebhyaḥ bhūyāt = let salutation (namaḥ) be offered (bhūyāt = may it be) to them; sakala su-janebhyaḥ = to all (sakala) good people (sujana); prati-dinam = every day.

contentBlocks.original:
इदमेकात्मतास्तोत्रं श्रद्धया यः सदा पठेत्‌ ।
स राष्ट्र धर्म निष्टावान्‌ अखण्डं भारतं स्मरेत्‌ ॥
contentBlocks.transliteration:
idamēkātmatāstōtraṃ śraddhayā yaḥ sadā paṭhēt‌ ।
sa rāṣṭra dharma niṣṭāvān‌ akhaṇḍaṃ bhārataṃ smarēt‌ ॥
contentBlocks.translation: One who recites this Ekātmata Stotra with devotion always becomes firm in national dharma and forever remembers undivided Bhārata.
contentBlocks.meanings: idam ekātmatā-stotram = this (idam) Ekātmata Stotra (hymn of unified consciousness); śraddhayā = with devotion/faith (śraddhā); yaḥ sadā paṭhet = one who always (sadā) recites (paṭhet = optative of paṭh); sa = he; rāṣṭra-dharma-niṣṭhāvān = one who is firm (niṣṭhāvān = possessed of steadfastness) in national dharma (rāṣṭra-dharma); akhaṇḍam bhāratam smaret = remembers (smaret) undivided (akhaṇḍa) Bhārata.
